title: Field notes — Northbrook Observatory
description: Staff dispatches from the dome — observing reports, telescope-time logs, equipment notes, and the occasional essay on what we look at and why.

sections.title: Dispatches from the dome.
sections.lede: What we looked at, what we saw, what surprised us, what the weather did. Updated when there is something to say — typically the week after a public observing night or after a research session.

background.alt: Editorial photograph of star-trails over the observatory ridge.

sections.text: Catching M51 in the spring drift.

The Whirlpool was due south at 22:40 local on the night of the May public — Ursa Major's tail high, transparency 0.91 mag/airmass per the boundary-layer sensor. We rotated the dome ten degrees east of meridian to clear a tree on the south horizon and started a 20-minute integration at 300mm focal-reducer. The companion NGC 5195 separates cleanly even on the live frame; six visitors stayed past the end of the night to watch the integration finish.

sections.caption: Single 20-minute stack of M51, faintly tinged warm by the bayer pattern at the CCD. The companion NGC 5195 is visible above-right of the primary spiral.

sections.text: What we saw of the Lyrids.

Twenty-one meteors in 90 minutes outdoors on the night of the 21st, ZHR-equivalent around 14 — short of the forecast 18, but the radiant was still climbing when transparency went down at 04:30. The brightest fireball at 03:51 left a five-second train; three of us had the same reaction at the same time, which is unusual. Recorded magnitudes are in the AAVSO log.

We've been folded into the AAVSO BSM Berry South campaign — the southern-hemisphere version was instrumented out, the northern version needed a second-site cross-check, and we have the right aperture, the right declination range, and (now) the right cadence. The campaign targets are a mix of eclipsing binaries with century-scale period drift and a handful of recurring novae that AAVSO would like a tighter outburst-detection net on. Public-night impact: none. The dome reads-out the full plate solve in 90 seconds, which means we can run BSM exposures BETWEEN public observing targets without interrupting visitors.

sections.body: We get less than 40 photometric nights a year. The campaign asks for 20. That math works.
sections.attribution: Hannah Reyes, in conversation with AAVSO

sections.text: The imager pipeline, rewritten in Rust.

We swapped the imaging pipeline from a Python pipeline that took two hours to plate-solve a night's frames to a Rust crate that does it in eight minutes on the same hardware. The science output is the same — bias / dark / flat / stack / wcs-fit / photometry on each AAVSO target — but the operator console now finishes before sunrise, so visitor prints can be ready by the next public night. Source is on the observatory's git mirror; happy to take pull requests.

The arctic outflow on the second of January dropped the surface dewpoint to -23°C and the boundary-layer aerosol to zero. The 21:00 SQM reading was 21.94 mag/arcsec² — Bortle 1, by the rounding rules — for the first time in nine years of continuous monitoring. The Milky Way cast a shadow on the snow at zenith. We left the dome closed and stood outside for forty minutes. Nobody said anything.
